Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

Small_molecule_information: (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol;sulfuric acid, also referred to as Paromomycin sulphate, is an aminoglycoside antibiotic existing as a sulfate salt, and Paromomycin sulphate is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, causing misreading of mRNA and inhibiting protein synthesis and is used to treat parasitic and bacterial infections, its core structure, (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, is similar to (2R,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(1R,2R,3S,4R,6S)-4,6-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,5R)-4-[(2R,3R,4R,5S,6S)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-4,5-dihydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, consisting of multiple aminosugar units linked via glycosidic bonds, featuring numerous amino and hydroxyl groups, the presence of multiple amino groups makes it strongly polycationic, which is the structural basis for its biological activity, allowing it to specifically bind to the bacterial ribosome and disrupt protein synthesis, its formulation as a sulfate salt is typical for this class of drugs, enhancing its water solubility and stability for clinical applications such as injection or solution preparation, this molecule is highly likely to be a clinically relevant antibiotic, such as a derivative of streptomycin, kanamycin, or tobramycin, employed as a broad-spectrum bactericidal agent primarily against aerobic Gram-negative infections, with caution required due to the risk of ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: Trisodium;5-oxo-1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-[(4-sulfonatophenyl)diazenyl]-4H-pyrazole-3-carboxylate, also referred to as Tartrazine (and known as the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)), is the chemical name for the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110), a synthetic azo dye with the formula C16H10N2Na3O7[(2R,3S,4S,5R,6R)-2-[(2R,3S,4S,5S,6S)-2-[(1R,2S)-3-[[(2R,3S,4S)-5-[[(2S,3R)-1-[2-[4-[4-[3-[4-[[2-[[2-[[2-[acetyl(propyl)amino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-prop-2-ynylamino]acetyl]amino]butylamino]propylcarbamoyl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]ethylamino]-3-hydroxy-1-oxobutan-2-yl]amino]-3-hydroxy-4-methyl-5-oxopentan-2-yl]amino]-2-[[6-amino-2-[(1S)-3-amino-1-[[(2S)-2,3-diamino-3-oxopropyl]amino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-methylpyrimidine-4-carbonyl]amino]-1-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-3-oxopropoxy]-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]oxy-3,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-4-yl]carbamate, and Tartrazine is a synthetic yellow azo dye used as a food coloring agent while primarily an industrial colorant, its structure features a pyrazolone core linked by an azo bond (NN) to two benzene rings, both substituted with sodium sulfonate groups, and exists as a trisodium salt, the azo structure imparts a vibrant color, in real-world use, Sunset Yellow is widely employed as an orange-yellow coloring agent in foods, beverages, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics, such as orange juice, sodas, candies, and ice cream, to enhance visual appeal, as a common food colorant, its use is strictly regulated globally to ensure safety within prescribed limits, despite some controversy regarding its potential to affect behavior in sensitive individuals, particularly children.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: actinium;2-amino-N-[2-[(2-amino-5-carbamimidamidopentanoyl)amino]-3-[3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]anilino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-carbamimidamidopentanamide, also referred to as [N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-2,3-diaminopropanamide] diargininamide (14b), is another radiopharmaceutical intended for Targeted Alpha Therapy, also containing the alpha-emitter Actinium (Ac) atom, and [N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-2,3-diaminopropanamide] diargininamide (14b) is a synthetic nucleobaseamino acid conjugate engineered to occupy the HIV-1 TAR RNA stembulgeloop in place of the viral Tat protein, its organic ligand is a longer, more complex peptide structure that includes two arginine residues (guanidine groups) and a scaffold linking to the methylamino thiazole aniline, it replaces the natural ribose-phosphate backbone with a metabolically stable thiazolearyl core and uses a 2,3-diaminopropanamide bridge to present two arginine side-chains for bidentate electrostatic contact with the TAR bulge, this polypeptide structure is typically designed for highly selective recognition and binding to specific receptors overexpressed on the surface of tumor cells, the complexity of the ligand enhances both its chelation stability for the radionuclide and its affinity for the biological target, making it a highly specific carrier for high-dose alpha-radiation delivery to tumor cells in clinical TAT, in fluorescence-binding assays 14b displays ultra-high affinity, adopts a 1:1 RNA-ligand stoichiometry, and retains good specificity against excess tRNA or dsDNA, in HIV-infected MAGIC-5B reporter cells 14b blocks Tat-mediated trans-activation with notable potency, suppresses viral replication comparably to a reference concentration of AZT, and shows no cytotoxicity up to a high tested concentration, these properties establish 14b as a metabolically stable, high-affinity artificial riboswitch ligand that selectively silences TAR-controlled transcription and constitutes a promising lead for next-generation RNA-targeted antivirals. Small_molecule_information: 2,3-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Nitidine, is another methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid, similar to 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium but with different methoxy substitution (2,3-positions), and as a benzophenanthridine alkaloid it also possesses antitumor and antimalarial activity, can inhibit topoisomerases and telomerase to disrupt DNA replication, and its fluorescent properties aid in cellular imaging studies; due to its planar, rigid, and positively charged structural features, its primary activity in practice is as a DNA intercalator and cytotoxic agent, exhibiting strong anti-tumor properties, minor changes in the substitution pattern (from 1,2- to 2,3-dimethoxy) are critical in drug design to modulate the molecules charge distribution and steric bulk, which can alter its interaction strength and selectivity for specific DNA sequences or Topoisomerase isozymes, and this makes it valuable in the structure-activity relationship studies of novel anticancer agents.

Small_molecule_information: (2S)-7-fluoro-2-methyl-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-10-oxo-4-oxa-1-azatricyclo[7.3.1.05,13]trideca-5(13),6,8,11-tetraene-11-carboxylic acid is a derivative of the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics. Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: sodium;4-amino-2-hydroxybenzoate;dihydrate, also referred to as 4-Aminosalicylate dihydrate, is the sodium salt dihydrate form of the compound p-Aminosalicylic acid (4-amino-2-hydroxybenzoic acid), with the molecular formula C7H6NNaO32H2O, consisting of a sodium ion, the p-Aminosalicylic acid anion, and two water molecules; it is a non-azaaromatic salicylic acid derivative, with an amino group (-NH) at the 4-position of the benzene ring and two water molecules as crystal water, lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and in terms of real-world activity, the sodium salt form is primarily used to increase the drugs solubility and bioavailability in water, making it more suitable for oral administration or intravenous injection, therefore, its pharmacological activity is identical to that of p-Aminosalicylic acid (PAS), whose core role is as an anti-tuberculosis drug, inhibiting bacterial folate synthesis (unrelated to azaaromatic sensing) and used clinically as a second-line antituberculosis drug for MDR-TB by competitively inhibiting PABA and blocking folic acid synthesis in Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

Small_molecule_information: 4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)benzenecarbothioamide, also referred to as 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole, is a combination of benzimidazole and carbothioamide structures, and 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole is a heterocyclic small molecule with a benzimidazole core linked to a thiocarbamoyl-substituted phenyl group, the benzimidazole core is often associated with antiparasitic, antifungal, antiviral, and antineoplastic activities, the carbothioamide group may enable the molecule to act as a metal chelator or enzyme inhibitor, the structure suggests the molecule may serve as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting on cell division or specific enzyme systems, unlike myricetin, it lacks reported affinity for CAG-repeat RNAs in HD/SCAs but may interact with structured RNAs via benzimidazole intercalation and thiocarbamoyl H-bonding, it shows no validated activity against polyQ aggregation or mitochondrial dysfunction in HD models, though its benzimidazole motif could potentially target nucleic acid mismatches, making it a speculative scaffold for TRED-focused optimization with further biophysical validation needed. -

Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as the saturated analog of DAPI (whose full name is 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide), is a compound featuring a dihydroindole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, with the formula C16H16N4, while DAPI is a fluorescent stain binding AT-rich DNA regions, widely used in microscopy for nuclear labeling and is cell-impermeant unless membranes are compromised, making it useful for viability assays and chromosome visualization; this compound is the saturated analog of 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, the saturation of the indole ring removes aromaticity and significantly increases the flexibility and changes the 3D shape of the molecule, this structural change dramatically influences its binding mode and affinity for the DNA minor groove, and this compound is investigated to explore the impact of molecular rigidity/flexibility on the activity of DNA-binding drugs, showing potential for anticancer and anti-infective activity.

Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

Small_molecule_information: (2S)-7-fluoro-2-methyl-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-10-oxo-4-oxa-1-azatricyclo[7.3.1.05,13]trideca-5(13),6,8,11-tetraene-11-carboxylic acid is a derivative of the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics. Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: Human A-site rRNA is a conserved structural motif within the small ribosomal subunit that forms the decoding center during mRNA translation. It can specifically recognize and bind to the anticodon stem-loop of aminoacyl-tRNA, ensuring accurate codon–anticodon pairing and maintaining translational fidelity. This RNA element also serves as a binding platform for aminoglycoside antibiotics, whose occupation disrupts decoding fidelity and leads to mis-translation, making the A-site rRNA a key regulator of protein synthesis homeostasis in human cells.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) commonly in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing, underpinning disease pathogenesis.

RNA information: r(GGCC)4 is a short, double-stranded RNA motif composed of four tandem GGCC repeats that adopts a fully base-paired hairpin structure. It is commonly used in competition assays as a negative control because it lacks internal loops or single-stranded regions and therefore does not bind to RNA-targeted small molecules such as CB253, which selectively recognize the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp RNA .

RNA information: cTNT (cardiac troponin T) pre-mRNA is a splicing reporter RNA that contains an alternatively spliced exon 5. It is regulated by the RNA-binding protein MBNL1, which binds upstream of exon 5 and represses its inclusion. In myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA sequesters MBNL1, leading to increased exon 5 inclusion in cTNT mRNA. Thus, cTNT splicing serves as a functional readout for MBNL1 activity and DM1-associated splicing defects in cellular models .

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazol-9-ol, also referred to as Compound 1 (a potent but non-selective 1a-derivative) and Compound 1e (a bis-benzimidazole derivative that closely resembles 1a in its 9-hydroxy-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazolium core but lacks the cationic piperidinium side chain, carrying instead a neutral 2-hydroxyethyl tail), is a tetracyclic aromatic heterocyclic compound based on the pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole scaffold, which forms the core of the well-known ellipticine alkaloid: the molecule is substituted with methyl groups at positions 5 and 11, and a hydroxyl group at position 9, and its planar conjugated system gives it fluorescence properties, with the molecular formula C19H16N2O; Compound 1 effectively inhibits RAN translation but also modestly affects canonical translation, indicating some off-target effects that limit its therapeutic potential, while Compound 1e has reduced affinity for the pathogenic 5CGG/3GGC internal loops of the r(CGG)exp RNA hairpin and weakened ability to disrupt the toxic r(CGG)12DGCR8 complex, leading to attenuated cellular efficacy (restoring partial wild-type SMN2 and Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells and achieving a modest reduction in the number of r(CGG)exp nuclear foci at a specific concentration) but retaining low cytotoxicity and good aqueous solubility, making it a useful negative-control probe for dissecting structureactivity relationships and validating the requirement of a permanent positive charge in next-generation ligands targeting CGG-repeat disorders; this compound and its derivatives are potent Topoisomerase II inhibitors and DNA intercalators, exhibiting strong anticancer activity.

Small_molecule_information: 9-methoxy-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 4, is a pyridocarbazole derivative structurally related to the powerful natural antitumor agent Ellipticine; it is an optimized, selective lead that potently binds the GG internal loop, selectively inhibits RAN translation without affecting canonical translation, prevents hnRNP H sequestration, and visualizes RNA foci in cells (establishing the hairpin as the therapeutically relevant target), while its large planar aromatic system facilitates DNA intercalation and topoisomerase inhibition, a characteristic that makes it a highly promising candidate in the development of new generation chemotherapeutic agents for various solid tumors.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[6-[(4-fluorobenzoyl)amino]-3-pyridinyl]pyridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound p7, is a linear heterocyclic compound composed of three aromatic rings (two pyridine rings and one phenyl ring) linked by two amide bonds; one pyridine ring is substituted at positions 3 and 6, connecting a pyridine-4-carboxamide group and a 4-fluorobenzoylamino group, and the incorporation of a fluorine atom (in the 4-fluorobenzoyl moiety) is a common strategy in medicinal chemistry used to fine-tune the molecules metabolic stability, lipophilicity, and affinity for its target, while the two amide bonds and three nitrogen heterocycles provide multiple hydrogen bond donor and acceptor sites, enhancing its potential for interaction with protein targets, and this multi-heterocyclic amide scaffold is frequently found in drug types such as kinase inhibitors and nuclear receptor ligands, suggesting that this compound or its analogues could be researched for anti-inflammatory or antitumor treatments, in addition, this compound is a small molecule identified through pharmacophore-based virtual screening as a selective ligand for the toxic RNA repeat expansion r(CUG)exp which is implicated in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), it was selected based on its alignment with a consensus pharmacophore model derived from known RNA-binding compounds particularly those targeting the CUG repeat RNA that sequesters MBNL1, a key splicing regulator, in vitro it effectively disrupts the r(CUG)12MBNL1 complex, and in cellular models it enhances nucleocytoplasmic transport of mRNA containing expanded CUG repeats as evidenced by increased luciferase activity, furthermore it improves DM1-associated splicing defects such as aberrant exon 5 inclusion in cardiac troponin T (cTNT) in a dose-dependent manner without affecting cell viability, notably it exhibits high selectivity for CUG-repeat RNA with minimal binding to other RNA or DNA structures, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing RNA-targeted therapeutics against DM1.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-methoxy-2-(4-methylbenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 8, is a rigid and structurally complex derivative of chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one (a fused chromenone-pyridine ring system) that features a methoxy group on the chromene ring, multiple substituents including 3-amino, 4-phenyl, 9-methoxy, and 2-(4-methylbenzoyl), and exhibited moderate RNA-binding behavior which helped illustrate the importance of substituent positioning on biological activity; Chromenone-based compounds are common in natural products and exhibit broad bioactivities, particularly anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticancer effects, and this highly substituted, complex heterocyclic system is often designed as a kinase inhibitor or receptor antagonist, with the benzoyl and phenyl substituents providing the necessary hydrophobic interactions and spatial orientation for protein target binding, while in drug research, such molecules are pursued for their unique structure and potential antitumor activity, possibly by interfering with the cell cycle or inhibiting specific signaling enzymes.

Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-4-thiophen-2-ylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 4, is a heterocyclic compound based on the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold. The key structural distinction lies in the substituent at position 4: a thiophen-2-yl group replaces the typical phenyl group. Thiophene is a five-membered sulfur-containing aromatic heterocycle, and its incorporation is a strategy of bioisosteric replacement, used to modify the substituents electronic effects and spatial conformation while retaining aromaticity. The large planar polycyclic structure of this compound still suggests its potential as a DNA intercalator or enzyme inhibitor, and featuring a thiophene substitution, it displayed moderate RNA-binding activity, which was less potent than the lead compounds but still showed specificity for CAG repeat RNA over control sequences. In practical applications, thiophene-containing drugs are often studied for anti-inflammatory, anti-tumor, and Central Nervous System diseases.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6: This RNA contains six consecutive CAG repeats with AA mismatches, forming multiple internal loops. It is a key target for small molecules due to its structural similarity to pathogenic expanded repeats. It shows high affinity and selective binding to compounds such as pyridocoumarin derivatives, leading to conformational changes and thermal stabilization.; The (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6 RNA contains multiple consecutive A-A mismatch loops. This multi-loop structure enhances conformational stability and ligand binding avidity, more accurately mimicking the expanded toxic repeats found in patient-derived RNA and providing a robust platform for biophysical testing.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-9-chloro-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 10, is a derivative of the complex chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, characterized by the 3-amino, 2-benzoyl, and 4-phenyl substituents. It features a chloro atom at position 9 (and overall contains a chlorine atom) and displayed significant binding affinity for CAG RNA, with effectiveness in gel shift and PCR stop assays indicating strong interaction with target RNA; the molecule is a highly rigid, planar polycyclic compound with multiple potential hydrogen bonding sites and stacking regions, and the chloro substitution serves to fine-tune the molecules physicochemical properties and steric hindrance. This fused polycyclic structure is an important template in anti-cancer drug design, with its pharmacological mechanism often hypothesized to involve DNA/RNA interference or inhibition of key enzymes regulating the cell cycle, and the chemical profile of this compound makes it a candidate for research as a cytotoxic agent or anti-proliferative drug.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x20: This is a control RNA with twenty repeats of perfectly paired CAG-GUC duplex. It is used to contrast with the mismatched x20 RNA and shows negligible binding and structural changes, underscoring the importance of the AA mismatch in selective compound recognition.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 3, shares the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core with 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one but lacks the bromine atom, and Compound 3 emerged as a lead compound with high affinity and selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, its large aromatic and amino-substituted structure suggests high lipophilicity and the potential to interact strongly with biological targets, it is likely investigated as an antiproliferative agent targeting intracellular enzymes or signaling pathways, Compound 3 significantly reduced polyQ aggregation in cellular models, showed low cytotoxicity, and was cell-permeable, localizing primarily in the cytoplasm. RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 3 (the first triazole analog tested, providing a stable and synthetically accessible replacement for olefin linkers), is a complex, linear peptidic analog, which can bind the HIV-1 FSS RNA and maintain affinity in the presence of competing tRNA, reduce HIV infectivity in pseudotyped systems and inhibit replication of the laboratory strain HIVIIIB with relatively low toxicity thus serving as a baseline compound in the triazole series; that is, there are no methyl groups on the phenylalanine amine and the two benzo[g]quinoline amide bonds, it incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline aromatic heterocycle and contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds, adopts a non-planar, flexible conformation, the benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the 3-aminopropylamine group enhances hydrophilicity and cationic properties, in terms of biological function, peptides and their analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands, enzyme inhibitors, or signaling molecules, and benzo[g]quinoline compounds typically possess diverse biological activities.

Small_molecule_information: 1-N,3-N-bis[4-(2,4,6-triaminopyrimidin-5-yl)butyl]benzene-1,3-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 11, which exhibits moderate inhibition potency and still effectively disrupts MBNL1-r(CCUG) foci in DM2 model cells while maintaining low cellular toxicity indicating good biocompatibility, possesses a sophisticated conformation, combining the non-linear, V-shaped structure of the benzene-1,3-dicarboximidamide core with the flexibility of two longer four-carbon butane chains, which connect the meta-imidamide groups to the terminal 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine rings. As a polycationic ligand with multiple basic imidamide and triaminopyrimidine groups, its research is typically focused on cell membrane interactions or selective nucleic acid binding. In medicinal chemistry, molecules with this structural pattern are considered excellent candidates for broad-spectrum antibacterial agents and cell-penetrating probes.

RNA information: TERRA (Telomeric Repeat-containing RNA) is a long non-coding RNA essential for telomere maintenance. It forms a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by three G-tetrads, which is more stable than non-canonical two-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies using reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays demonstrate that TERRA's rG4 causes significant stalling (~40%) under potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, confirming its stable formation. This structural integrity is crucial for its role in telomere length regulation and heterochromatin formation. Notably, ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS act as effective molecular chaperones, significantly inducing and stabilizing the TERRA rG4 structure even under unfavorable ionic conditions (Li⁺), highlighting its dynamic nature and functional importance in genome stability.; TERRA (telomeric repeat-containing RNA) is a well-characterized RNA that forms stable G-quadruplex structures, with its sequence featuring repeated G-tracts . It binds selectively to RGB-1, showing a 1:1.2 binding stoichiometry and inducing a Tₘ increase of the RNA G-quadruplex upon binding. As a 5'UTR insert in reporter mRNAs, it mediates translation repression by RGB-1 in vitro and HEK293 cells, serving as a model for studying RNA G-quadruplex-related translational regulation.

RNA information: TERRA (Telomeric Repeat-containing RNA) is a long non-coding RNA essential for telomere maintenance. It forms a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by three G-tetrads, which is more stable than non-canonical two-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies using reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays demonstrate that TERRA's rG4 causes significant stalling (~40%) under potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, confirming its stable formation. This structural integrity is crucial for its role in telomere length regulation and heterochromatin formation. Notably, ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS act as effective molecular chaperones, significantly inducing and stabilizing the TERRA rG4 structure even under unfavorable ionic conditions (Li⁺), highlighting its dynamic nature and functional importance in genome stability.; TERRA (telomeric repeat-containing RNA) is a well-characterized RNA that forms stable G-quadruplex structures, with its sequence featuring repeated G-tracts . It binds selectively to RGB-1, showing a 1:1.2 binding stoichiometry and inducing a Tₘ increase of the RNA G-quadruplex upon binding. As a 5'UTR insert in reporter mRNAs, it mediates translation repression by RGB-1 in vitro and HEK293 cells, serving as a model for studying RNA G-quadruplex-related translational regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 5-(furan-2-yl)-2-methyl-3H-thieno[2,3-d]pyrimidin-4-one, also referred to as Molecule 2, is a compound containing thienopyrimidine and furan heterocyclic rings which is a half-fragment analog of RGB-1, lacking RGB-1s symmetric structure; its structure is based on the thienopyrimidine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyrimidine ring, substituted with a keto group at position 4, a methyl group at position 2, and a furan-2-yl group at position 5, and unlike RGB-1, it shows no detectable binding affinity or stabilizing effect on RNA G-quadruplexes (e.g., TERRA) or other nucleic acid structures (DNA G-quadruplexes, stem-loop/dsRNA), with no impact on their melting temperatures, has no effect on RTase elongation for TERRA-containing or non-structured RNA, and fails to alter translation of G-quadruplex-containing mRNAs or NRAS protein expression in cells, serving as a negative control; in terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically exhibits biological activities such as antibacterial, antifungal, or anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide;dibromide, also referred to as PhenDH4, which contains a central phenanthroline core with 1-ethylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and demonstrated good G4 binding but no biological effect, incorporates the large, aromatic 1,10-phenanthroline core, a structure well-known in coordination chemistry as a powerful transition metal ion chelator, this core is symmetrically linked to two permanently charged quinolinium fragments, the significance of 1,10-phenanthroline lies in its ability to form a perfect bidentate pocket for metal ions like Fe2 or Cu2, consequently, 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide;dibromide likely exhibits a dual mechanism of action: it acts as a bis-cationic DNA intercalator and as a metal chelator, potentially disrupting cellular metal homeostasis or generating reactive oxygen species via metal complexes, such molecules are often investigated as highly potent multi-target anticancer agents.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine, also referred to as the molecule featuring a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core with the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and an ethane-1,2-diamine side chain at the 11-position, is a primary amine analogue, featuring the ethane-1,2-diamine side chain, which demonstrated strong binding and potent anti-proliferative effects in A375 cells; the primary amine functions as a hydrogen bond donor and coordination site, influencing its metabolic stability and interaction profile with biological targets compared to tertiary amine analogues, and this compound is used in the study of nucleic acid binding and cytotoxic activity and is relevant in metal complexation research.

Small_molecule_information: 11-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline, also referred to as the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline with the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl side chain at the 11-position, features the [1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core, known for its high planarity, conjugation, and strong fluorescence; the 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl group is directly attached at position 11, a strongly basic substituent used to confer antiproliferative activity and modulate receptor affinity, though this compound had weak binding affinity for the NRQ and no anti-proliferative activity at the highest tested concentration (50 M) in any cell line, and the planar, highly conjugated molecule is used in anticancer drug development and as a potential fluorescent dye for cellular imaging.

Small_molecule_information: N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as an indolo[3,2-b]quinoline with the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 3-position and a 2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, is a neutral indolo[3,2-b]quinoline with an N-methylpiperazine side chain; this design aims to balance good membrane permeability (due to the neutral amine) with improved solubility and target affinity (due to the piperazine), and the compound demonstrated binding affinity for the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex while showing good anti-proliferative activity, and it is investigated as a potential antiproliferative agent.

Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of 4b-2, is the quaternary ammonium salt based on the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. It is designed as a potent cytotoxic agent, likely acting as a DNA intercalator, and is investigated for its structure-activity relationship in antitumor drug development; this derivative is one of the strongest binders in its series for the NRQ, exhibits strong anti-proliferative activity in A375, yet has poor selectivity due to its binding to mutant and hairpin oligomers.

RNA information: A designed Watson–Crick stem-loop RNA with a 7-bp stem and 4-nt loop (AGUG). It serves as a non-G4 control structure. Ligands 2a and 2b bind to it with lower affinity , confirming their specificity for G4 structures.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione, also referred to as a thiophene-fused analogue of 2a with similar side chains, is an organic compound featuring a complex polycyclic core of naphthoquinone and benzothiole, forming the naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione skeleton. This framework is a sulfur-containing heteroaromatic system with a highly conjugated electron structure and redox-active quinone carbonyl groups at positions 5 and 10. The substituents at positions 4 and 11 are two 2-aminoethylamino groups, which introduce primary amines capable of protonation, granting the molecule polycationic character and increasing its water solubility under physiological conditions. This specific structural combination, characterized by its planarity, extended conjugation, redox activity, and cationic side chains, makes it a molecule of interest in biomedicine, typically investigated as a potential DNA intercalator or topoisomerase inhibitor, suggesting potential cytotoxic or anticancer activities; it has moderate cellular uptake, stabilizes RG4s, and also suppresses translation and promotes apoptosis, though less potently than 2a.

RNA information: Located at positions 16–30 in the KRAS 5′-UTR. It forms a stable parallel RNA G-quadruplex (RG4), melting temperature (Tm) of 53°C, and ΔG of –4.9 kcal/mol. It is recognized by G4-specific antibodies and stabilized by small molecules like 2a and 2b.

Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione, also referred to as a furan-fused anthraquinone derivative with bis(2-aminoethylamino) side chains, is a compound based on a Naphthoquinone core fused with a Benzofuran ring, resulting in a Naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione structure, substituted with two 2-aminoethylamino groups at positions C-4 and C-11. Anthraquinone/Naphthoquinone compounds are a well-known class of DNA topoisomerase inhibitors and DNA intercalators, forming the basis of many chemotherapy drugs (e.g., Mitoxantrone). Additionally, these quinone structures readily undergo redox cycling, generating Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) that cause DNA damage and cell apoptosis, and this compound also exhibits high cellular uptake, strong stabilization of KRAS RG4s and specific binding affinity, which enables it to effectively inhibit translation and induce apoptosis in pancreatic cancer cells, meaning it is therefore primarily researched as a novel anti-tumor agent with a mechanism involving dual DNA intercalation and ROS-mediated cytotoxicity.

RNA information: It forms a stable RG4 structure, contributing to translational inhibition.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[2-[1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium-2-yl]ethenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 20, is a mono-cationic quinolinium dye, similar to a related molecule but with only a phenolic hydroxyl group on the phenyl ring; this compound is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 4-hydroxystyryl group at C2 and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl moiety at C4 of the quinoline ring, synthesized via general procedure B, and its hydroxyl group confers pH sensitivity to the molecules fluorescence, while its 4-hydroxystyryl group enables partial RNA interaction, though it is less potent than analogs with carbazolyl/indolyl groups, failing to robustly boost ADAM10 expression or reduce A production; due to its permanent positive charge and pH-responsive nature, it is commonly designed as an organelle-selective fluorescent probe to target and monitor the pH of acidic compartments like the nucleus or mitochondria, and it binds ADAM10 mRNAs QGRS with good affinity and shows weak translational up-regulation in dual-luciferase assays, in addition, its structure also gives it strong DNA-binding capabilities, leading to research into its potential as an anticancer agent.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[2-[2-(9-ethylcarbazol-3-yl)ethenyl]-1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl]piperazin-1-yl]-N,N-dimethylethanamine, also referred to as Compound 26 (a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 2-(2-(9-ethyl-9H-carbazol-3-yl)vinyl) group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring and a 4-(2-methoxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl group at the 4-position), belongs to the carbazole-quinolinium cationic series, differentiated by its side chain length: a N,N-dimethylethanamine group linked to the piperazine ring, making it one carbon shorter than a similar analogue; for this compound, SPR assay indicates its affinity for A10-RNA-Wt is lower than that of Compound 24, it was not subjected to the dual-luciferase reporter assay so its impact on ADAM10 translation remains unreported, and no clear cytotoxicity data is provided for it in the tested compound tables, while in medicinal chemistry, this subtle variation in linker length is a critical strategy to fine-tune a molecules lipophilicity, basicity, and spatial orientation, which collectively dictate its cell permeability and target binding mode, consequently, it would be systematically compared with its homologues in applications such as nucleic acid probing or antineoplastic activity to identify the optimal chain length that maximizes the therapeutic index or probe performance.

Small_molecule_information: 9-ethyl-3-[(E)-2-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 16, is a simpler carbazole-quinoline fluorescent dye with high quantum yield, which is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a (9-ethyl-9H-carbazol-2-yl)vinyl group at the 2-position (no 4-position amine substitution) synthesized via general procedure B, binds ADAM10 mRNAs QGRS with moderate affinity and low mutant RNA interaction, weakly up-regulates ADAM10 translation in dual-luciferase assays and is less potent than analogs with 4-methylpiperazine due to lacking this motif for optimal RNA G-quadruplex binding, and is used in applications like dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) or as biological probes for cell membranes.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[2-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 12, is a green-yellow quaternary ammonium dye linking 1-methylquinolin-1-ium with a p-hydroxyphenyl group, which is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 4-hydroxystyryl group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring and no substitution at the 4-position; the phenolic hydroxyl group is an electron donor whose protonation/deprotonation is pH-dependent, consequently, this compound is used as a bio-pH sensitive fluorescent probe for monitoring acidity/alkalinity in cellular or tissue environments, and its cationic planar structure also grants it nucleic acid binding and antimicrobial potential, while SPR assay shows it has weak affinity for A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence), dual-luciferase reporter assay reveals it exerts little effect on ADAM10 translation, and it exhibits cytotoxicity.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[2-(1-methyl-4-piperidin-1-ylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-4-nitrophenol, also referred to as Compound 7, is a typical quinolinium polymethine dye and a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 2-hydroxy-5-nitrostyryl group at C2 and a piperidin-1-yl moiety at C4 of the quinoline ring, synthesized via general procedure B, its core is the quinoline ring, methylated at N1 to form a quaternary ammonium salt, making it permanently positively charged, the C2 position is linked via a vinyl bridge to a nitrophenol ring, and C4 is substituted with a piperidine ring, this structure possesses an extended conjugated system, characteristic of highly efficient fluorescent dyes, its cationic nature predisposes it to target negatively charged cellular components, such as mitochondria (due to their negative membrane potential) or cell membranes, it primarily applies in bioimaging as a fluorescent indicator for membrane potential or a fluorescent tracer for specific organelles in biological and medical research, meanwhile, it binds ADAM10 mRNAs QGRS weakly and shows no translational up-regulation, its piperidine (vs methylpiperazine) and nitro-substituted styryl group hinder optimal RNA G-quadruplex interactions, lacking potency to enhance ADAM10 expression.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide dibromide, also referred to as PhenDH5, is another derivative in the 2-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide dibromide/4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide dibromide series, this compound is similar to PhenDH3 but with 1-benzylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups, showed moderate G4 affinity and no effect on EBNA1, with poor drug-likeness, and it has a significantly more extended central scaffold: 1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide, phenanthroline is a rigid tricyclic aromatic heterocycle (fused benzene and two pyridine rings) known for its excellent planarity, extended conjugation, and its nitrogen atoms at positions 1 and 10 serving as common metal ion coordination sites, this compound is a dicationic system with two quaternized quinoline side chains and bromide counterions, the phenanthroline core dramatically increases the molecules planarity and conjugation length, resulting in a very large planar aromatic molecule, this structure means that, in addition to its potential for DNA minor groove binding, it likely possesses DNA intercalation capabilities, allowing it to insert between DNA base pairs, furthermore, the phenanthroline core can coordinate with transition metals (such as Cu2 or Fe2) to form redox-active metal complexes, thus, this type of compound and its metal complexes are studied as novel anticancer agents with mechanisms potentially involving DNA binding, intercalation, or the generation of oxidative stress.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.

RNA information: MALAT1 is a well-studied long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) known to form a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure consisting of three G-tetrads. It functions as a key regulatory molecule in processes such as alternative splicing modulation. The rG4 structure within MALAT1 has been confirmed in vitro through techniques including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays and fluorescence-based methods using probes like NMM and ThT. Studies show that in potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, MALAT1's rG4 forms efficiently, causing significant reverse transcriptase stalling (~60%). Notably, ligands such as PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, inducing and stabilizing MALAT1's rG4 structure even under unfavorable lithium (Li⁺) conditions, highlighting its dynamic structural plasticity and potential as a therapeutic target.

RNA information: RPPH1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) that can form a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by only two G-tetrads, which distinguishes it from canonical rG4s that typically consist of three or more tetrads. This structural configuration contributes to its role in transcriptional regulation. Functionally, RPPH1 has been identified as a regulatory RNA molecule, and its rG4 structure can be stabilized or induced by specific small molecule ligands like **PhenDC3 and PDS. In vitro analyses, including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, demonstrate that the RPPH1 rG4 structure causes approximately 13% stalling efficiency in potassium-containing buffers, confirming its ability to form a stable secondary structure that can impede enzymatic activity. The integrity of this rG4 structure is crucial for its biological function, particularly in the context of gene expression regulation.

RNA information: XIST is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) crucial for X-chromosome inactivation in female mammalian cells. It functions by coating the future inactive X chromosome and recruiting repressive complexes to silence gene expression. Structurally, XIST contains a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) motif composed of only two G-tetrads, making it less stable than canonical three-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies, such as reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, confirm its ability to form an rG4 structure, showing approximately 14% stalling in potassium buffer. Notably, specific G4 ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, significantly enhancing the formation and stability of the XIST rG4 structure under unfavorable ionic conditions, highlighting its dynamic nature and regulatory potential.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[3-(dimethylamino)-2-hydroxypropyl]-4,6-dimethyl-4H-benzimidazole-5,7-dione, also referred to as Xanthinol (7-[2-hydroxy-3-[2-hydroxyethyl(methyl)amino]propyl]-1,3-dimethylpurine-2,6-dione), has a core of 4,6-dimethyl-4H-benzimidazole-5,7-dione, a structure that contains both a benzimidazole (a nitrogen heterocycle) and a quinonoid diketone (a redox-active moiety), while Xanthinol is a theophylline derivative designed to serve as a neutral, membrane-permeant surrogate of the ribityl phosphate moiety of flavin mononucleotide (FMN) which by replacing the anionic ribose phosphate with a cationic 2-hydroxy-3-[2-hydroxyethyl(methyl)amino]propyl side-chain acquires enhanced lipophilicity and metabolic stability while preserving the hydrogen-bonding face of the xanthine ring, can completely disrupt the TatTARcyclin T1 complex in electrophoretic mobility-shift assays, dose-dependently inhibits Tat-driven LTR trans-activation without affecting a heterologous Rev-RRE control in HeLa reporter assays, and induces large chemical-shift perturbations exclusively in the 5 bulge even at sub-stoichiometric ratios in NMR titrations, confirming RNA-specific recognition analogous to FMN-riboswitch binding thus providing a non-phosphate, non-peptide scaffold for antiviral agents that target viral RNA instead of host proteins, and the N1 position of 1-[3-(dimethylamino)-2-hydroxypropyl]-4,6-dimethyl-4H-benzimidazole-5,7-dione is substituted with a 3-(dimethylamino)-2-hydroxypropyl side chain, which includes a tertiary amine that can be protonated at physiological pH, carrying a positive charge, and a secondary alcohol, with the quinonoid diketone part suggesting redox activity, potentially exerting biological effects by generating reactive oxygen species or participating in electron transfer mechanisms, and this class of molecules is often studied as antimalarial or cytotoxic anti-cancer drugs.

bis-cyclopentyl amidine-substituted biphenyl furan structure, and DB244 is a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative that belongs to the same chemotype as DB60 and DB75 but is endowed with bulkier cyclopentylamidine end-groups, furan-phenyl scaffolds are often used to construct fluorescent probes or molecules with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, the bis-amidine substitution suggests potential DNA binding or serine protease inhibitory activity, with the cyclopentyl substitution likely intended to enhance lipophilicity and cell membrane permeability, DB244 was originally footprints reveal protection of the GCUCU site opposite the bulge only at high concentrations, confirming weaker, lower-occupancy binding, like DB60, DB244 recognizes the three-dimensional cavity created by the pyrimidine bulge rather than specific nucleotides: mutation of U23 or the GC26-39 pair has little influence on affinity, whereas deletion of the bulge abolishes the interaction, the Small_molecule_information: N-pentan-3-yl-4-[5-[4-(N-pentan-3-ylcarbamimidoyl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as DB226 (2,5-bis-[4-(N-isopentylamidino)phenyl]furan), is a bis-amidine compound containing biphenyl and furan structures, which is a bis-cationic diphenylfuran congener in which the terminal amidines carry branched isopentyl side-chains; its structure is based on the benzene and furan parent skeletons: two benzene rings are linked by a furan ring, and the para-position (position 4) of each benzene ring is substituted with an N-pentan-3-ylcarboximidamide group; in terms of biological function, amidine compounds are often used as antiparasitic agents (such as for Trypanosoma and Plasmodium), antibacterial agents, or as DNA binding agents, and this compound was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii (PCP) therapy, with its lipophilic modification strengthening minor-groove binding to AT-rich DNA and preserving high anti-PCP potency in rat models, while when tested against the HIV-1 TAR RNA target, the extra bulk reduces RNA affinity, showing no measurable suppression of Tat-driven trans-activation in the relevant cellular assay, and melting-temperature experiments record a modest Tsubm/sub for the DB226TAR complex, with RNase A footprints requiring a certain concentration for detectable protection of the GCUCU site opposite the bulge, confirming low-occupancy binding; like its analogues, DB226 recognizes the three-dimensional cavity created by the pyrimidine bulge rather than specific nucleotidesmutation of certain sites has little influence, whereas deletion of the bulge abolishes interaction; the compound is non-toxic to reporter cells below a specific concentration and retains full anti-PCP efficacy, illustrating that enlarging the alkyl-amidine substituent tips selectivity toward DNA while diminishing RNA recognition, thus, although DB226 itself is not an effective anti-HIV lead, its profile further defines structureselectivity relationships guiding the design of next-generation TAR-targeted diphenylfurans.
